fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a codec library for the real-time messaging layer we're building. Basically the idea is that our app developers shouldn't have to worry about the low-level wire format stuff — they just say 'send this event to this namespace with these args' and the library handles everything underneath. We had some complaints recently that people are getting subtly wrong frames when they hand-assemble these strings, especially around binary data and the multi-version support (remember how we handled that versioning split in the auth module last time? something similar applies here). The library needs to handle building connection URLs, encoding outbound messages of various kinds, and decoding incoming frames back into structured data. There's also something around binary attachments that needs to be tracked across multiple incoming frames before a message is considered complete. One thing that keeps coming up in complaints is that ports in URLs sometimes show up when they shouldn't, or get dropped when they should be kept — we need that to be airtight. Output should be line-oriented key=value pairs. Please keep the code organized across multiple files, not one big blob. The exact rules for how the v3 vs v4 protocol differences work for connect frames are a bit fuzzy to me, so make sure whoever picks this up digs into the spec carefully.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: string Encode(Command cmd) / Message Decode(Frame[] frames)

trigger_keywords: when to keep or strip the port number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Port handling depends on the scheme AND the actual port value together. For http/ws schemes, port 80 is the default and must be dropped. For https/wss schemes, port 443 is the default and must be dropped. A port that does NOT match the default for its scheme must always be kept — for example, https on port 80 keeps `:80`, and http on port 443 keeps `:443`. This logic applies uniformly regardless of the transport (websocket or polling).

trigger_keywords: how does auth differ between versions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Under protocol v3: the default namespace (null/empty) produces NO frame at all — output `frame=null`. A named namespace produces `40<namespace>` optionally followed by `?key=value` query params, then a trailing comma (e.g., `40/test?key=value,`). Auth is not embedded. Under protocol v4: the default namespace ALWAYS produces a connect frame `40`. A named namespace produces `40<namespace>,`. An auth object is serialized as compact JSON appended directly after the prefix or after `<namespace>,` (e.g., `40{"userId":1}` or `40/test,{"userId":1}`). Query parameters are NOT embedded in the v4 connect frame — they belong only in the handshake URL.

When any argument in an event or ack contains binary data (represented as `{"$bytes": [...]}` in the input), each such value must be lifted out into a separate ordered attachment. It is replaced inline with `{"_placeholder":true,"num":N}` where N is the zero-based attachment index in argument order. The frame prefix changes: plain event `42` becomes `45`, plain ack `43` becomes `46`. The text frame is also prefixed with `<count>-` immediately after the type digits (e.g., `451-` for one attachment). Each attachment is emitted as a separate `attachment=<comma-separated byte values>` line after the `frame=` line.

A binary frame (type `45` or `46`) spans multiple incoming frames: one text frame declaring the attachment count, followed by that many raw byte-array frames. The message must NOT be emitted until all declared attachment frames have arrived. Until then, nothing is output. Once all attachments arrive, emit `type=binary_event` or `type=binary_ack` with the event name (for binary_event), optional namespace, optional id, `attachment_count=<N>`, and `data=<JSON array with placeholders still in place, minus the event name element for binary_event>`.

In decoded message output: a `namespace=` line is emitted ONLY when the namespace is non-default (i.e., not null/empty/`/`). An `id=` line is emitted ONLY when the frame carried an acknowledgement id. For event messages, the event name is removed from the data array before rendering `data=`; the remaining elements form the data array. For ack messages, the full stored array is rendered as-is. Unrecognized or empty frames produce `message=none` with no other fields.

The output URL scheme follows the transport flag, not the input scheme. If `ws=true` (websocket transport): `http` input maps to `ws` output, `https` maps to `wss`, and `ws`/`wss` stay as-is. If `ws=false` (polling transport): `ws` input maps to `http`, `wss` maps to `https`, and `http`/`https` stay as-is. The fixed query string is always `EIO=<version>&transport=polling` or `EIO=<version>&transport=websocket`, followed by any additional supplied query params as `&key=value`. The mount path defaults to `/socket.io` when empty or not supplied; the path is followed by a trailing `/` before `?`.

For `extract_payload` with type `Event`: the input text is a JSON array whose first element is the event name. Strip the first element and render only the remaining elements as a compact JSON array (e.g., `["event",1]` → `payload=[1]`). For type `Ack`: render the full array as-is without removing any element (e.g., `["event","hello",{...}]` → `payload=["event","hello",{...}]`). Output is a single line `payload=<compact JSON array>`.

The implementation must be split across multiple files reflecting distinct responsibilities: URL construction, outbound encoding (event, ack, connect), inbound decoding, attachment/binary handling, payload rendering, and a stdin/stdout adapter. The adapter is the only component that reads JSON from stdin and writes key=value lines to stdout; the codec core must be fully decoupled from I/O. The adapter translates each JSON command into idiomatic calls on the codec API and formats the result. No single file should combine more than one major concern.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'handshake_url', 'encode_event', 'encode_event_ack', 'encode_ack', 'encode_connect', 'decode', 'extract_payload'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled that versioning split in the auth module last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The protocol v3 vs v4 divergence in encode_connect behavior: v3 omits default-namespace frames and embeds query params inline; v4 always sends a connect frame for default namespace and embeds auth as JSON instead. See feature2_4_encode_connect.json and the corresponding section in start.md.

context_pointers.vague_reference: ports in URLs sometimes show up when they shouldn't, or get dropped when they should be kept
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The scheme-relative default port rule: port 80 is dropped for http/ws, port 443 is dropped for https/wss, but a port that doesn't match the scheme's default (e.g., https on port 80) must be explicitly preserved. See feature1_handshake_url.json test case 4 and the port-handling description in start.md Feature 1.

context_pointers.vague_reference: something around binary attachments that needs to be tracked across multiple incoming frames before a message is considered complete
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The binary decode state machine in feature3_2_decode_binary.json: a `45`/`46` text frame declares attachment count N, then N subsequent byte-array frames must be collected before the decoded message is emitted. The codec must buffer the partial state silently.

context_pointers.vague_reference: output should be line-oriented key=value pairs
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The adapter output format: each decoded or encoded result is rendered as one `key=value` line per field (e.g., `type=event`, `event=hi`, `namespace=/test`, `data=[...]`), with multiple decoded messages separated by a `---` line, and `message=none` for unrecognized frames. See feature3_1_decode_text.json and the rendering rules in start.md Feature 3.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in the current ticket. You'll need to look at the existing protocol spec references and the test cases in the rcb_tests folder — they serve as the contract for the expected behavior. If something seems ambiguous, align with whatever the test cases demonstrate and flag any gaps for review.

feature1_handshake_url.json.description: Build the Engine.IO handshake/connection URL from a server endpoint, transport choice (long-polling vs websocket), protocol version, optional mount path and query parameters. Default ports for the scheme are dropped; a non-default port for the scheme is preserved.

feature2_1_encode_event.json.description: Encode an outbound event message with an event name, optional namespace and an arbitrary argument list. Plain values are inlined as a JSON array prefixed by the event name; binary arguments are replaced by placeholder objects ({"_placeholder":true,"num":N}) and emitted as separate ordered attachment payloads, switching the frame prefix to the binary form.

feature2_2_encode_event_ack.json.description: Encode an outbound event message that also requests an acknowledgement: the same event encoding as a plain event, but the numeric acknowledgement id is inserted directly before the JSON argument array (after the namespace, when present). Binary arguments still become placeholders plus ordered attachments.

feature2_3_encode_ack.json.description: Encode an outbound acknowledgement message replying to a previously received id-bearing request. There is no event name; the argument array is emitted directly, preceded by the acknowledgement id (and namespace when present). Binary arguments become placeholders plus ordered attachments and switch the frame to the binary-ack prefix.

feature2_4_encode_connect.json.description: Encode the namespace-connection frame. Under protocol v3 the default namespace produces no frame (none is sent), while a named namespace yields a connect frame that carries the namespace and, optionally, the connection query string. Under protocol v4 the default namespace always produces a connect frame, an auth object is serialized inline as JSON, and connection queries are not embedded in this frame.

feature3_1_decode_text.json.description: Decode a single inbound text frame into a structured message. The numeric prefix selects the kind (heartbeat ping/pong, transport opened handshake, namespace connected/disconnected, event, acknowledgement, connect error). Optional namespace, acknowledgement id, event name and JSON argument payload are extracted from their wire positions; unrecognized or empty frames yield no message.

feature3_2_decode_binary.json.description: Decode an inbound binary message that spans a leading text frame plus one or more raw attachment frames. The text frame declares the attachment count, optional namespace, optional acknowledgement id and the JSON payload (with binary placeholders). The message is only produced once all declared attachment frames have arrived; until then nothing is emitted.

cases.expected_output:
type=binary_event
event=1 params
namespace=/test
attachment_count=1
data=[{"_placeholder":true,"num":0}]

type=binary_event
event=1 params
id=30
attachment_count=1
data=[{"_placeholder":true,"num":0}]

feature4_extract_payload.json.description: Render the JSON arguments array carried by a decoded message back to a compact JSON string. For an event message the leading event-name element has already been consumed, so only the remaining arguments are rendered; for an acknowledgement message the full stored array is rendered as-is.
